video_title: SPECIAL REPORT: Will The Fertilizer Shortage Create A Global Food Crisis? | Bruce Sherrick
gist: University of Illinois land economist Bruce Sherrick argues the Strait of Hormuz closure and tariffs delivered a fertilizer/energy PRICE shock, not a physical supply shock, for the northern hemisphere this harvest — most fertilizer was already in the ground — so he is NOT ringing a global-famine alarm for fall 2026. His real worries are: a prolonged conflict spilling into a wider regional war, food-price-driven insecurity in Africa and western parts of Asia, and depressed US commodity prices from trade rerouting. His central investment thesis is farmland as the most-fixed, inflation-hedging, equity-uncorrelated asset poised for a structural demand wave from the $126T boomer wealth transfer.

themes.theme: Hormuz/Tariff shock is price, not physical supply (this harvest)

insights.claim: The Strait of Hormuz closure, through which roughly one-third of the world's seaborne fertilizer trade passes, will NOT cause a northern-hemisphere fertilizer shortage for the 2026 fall harvest because most of the required fertilizer had already shipped and was applied before the closure (a 'pig in the python' lag).
insights.speaker: guest, Bruce Sherrick (U. Illinois land economist)
insights.so_what: Fades panic-driven longs in fertilizer/ag on a 'famine this fall' narrative; the actionable shock is price/margin, not tonnage availability, for the current cycle.
insights.quote: it's sort of a pig in the python problem. It takes a long time to work something through anyhow and most of the fertilizer was already in place for the northern hemisphere planting season this year

insights.claim: Much of the US corn crop's nitrogen was applied last fall as anhydrous ammonia and most dry fertilizers were already contracted and in place before spring, so 2026 northern-hemisphere planting was largely insulated from the Hormuz disruption.
insights.speaker: guest, Bruce Sherrick
insights.so_what: The window for a physical-shortage trade in 2026 northern-hemisphere inputs has effectively closed; watch the NEXT application cycle (fall 2026 / southern-hemisphere) instead.
insights.quote: much of the nitrogen application that was required for the corn crop in the US was done last fall

insights.claim: Fertilizer prices rose sharply from a combination of the Hormuz closure, Canada/Mexico tariffs that pre-dated the closure, and higher energy prices that feed directly into ammonia production — but unlike the 2022 Russia-Ukraine shock there was NO offsetting rise in output/commodity prices.
insights.speaker: guest, Bruce Sherrick
insights.so_what: Pure input-cost squeeze on farmer margins with no commodity-price tailwind — bearish for US grower net income and a margin headwind for grain-dependent processors.
insights.quote: the net effect is that the input prices went up quite a bit. There's not a real material response on what you might think of as the output prices

insights.claim: In 2022 the Russia-Ukraine war raised input costs but also lifted commodity prices (especially wheat, sympathetically corn and soybeans) because Black Sea grain exports were cut; the Hormuz case is different because virtually no commodities flow through Hormuz, only energy and fertilizer.
insights.speaker: guest, Bruce Sherrick
insights.so_what: Don't model the Hormuz event off the 2022 playbook — there is no automatic grain-price hedge for farmers this time, so the net P&L effect on growers is worse.

insights.claim: Higher energy prices transmit into agriculture faster and more directly than fertilizer prices, with diesel costs described as 'really really high right now,' so energy should always be paired with fertilizer when assessing the food-cost shock.
insights.speaker: guest, Bruce Sherrick
insights.so_what: Energy is the faster-moving channel into food costs; oil/diesel price relief would ease ag input inflation more quickly than fertilizer relief.
insights.quote: whenever we talk about fertilizer we should continue to say and energy because diesel costs are really really high right now as well

themes.theme: Fertilizer supply mechanics, dependency, and inelasticity

insights.claim: The US imports only ~13% of its nitrogen and ~16% of its phosphate, whereas Brazil imports ~95% of nitrogen and ~72% of phosphate — and BOTH countries import essentially 100% of their potassium (potash).
insights.speaker: guest, Bruce Sherrick
insights.so_what: Brazil is far more exposed to a fertilizer trade disruption than the US; potash is the universal import-dependency choke point for both — watch potash producers and Brazilian ag input costs.
insights.quote: The US imports about 13% of its nitrogen. Brazil imports about 95%

insights.claim: Fertilizer supply is highly inelastic: a new large-scale plant is a multi-year project, existing plants run near full utilization (not at 60% spare capacity), and nitrogen in particular is a near-real-time system locked to seasonal application needs.
insights.speaker: guest, Bruce Sherrick
insights.so_what: Supply cannot ramp to backfill interrupted shipments quickly, so any prolonged disruption is a durable price tailwind for incumbent producers with available capacity.
insights.quote: you can't just put in a large scale fertilizer plant in a you know, that's not even a one-year project. That's a big project

insights.claim: Mined nutrients (phosphate, potash — 'dig it out of the ground and grind it up') have somewhat more flexible production than nitrogen, but nitrogen output is essentially fixed and cannot be ramped dramatically.
insights.speaker: guest, Bruce Sherrick
insights.so_what: Differentiates producers: mined-nutrient miners have marginally more flex; nitrogen producers benefit most from price spikes since supply is rigid.

insights.claim: Russian fertilizer/ammonia supply out of the Black Sea is partially but not fully restored — some ammonia pipelines and transmission channels were destroyed and have not been rebuilt, and the condition of Odessa's deep-water ports is uncertain.
insights.speaker: guest, Bruce Sherrick
insights.so_what: A persistent structural reduction in global ammonia/fertilizer export capacity supports a higher baseline price floor than pre-2022.
insights.quote: there were a couple of ammonia um pipelines that have not been restored

insights.claim: Rationing and crop-mix shifts are the likely adjustment to tight fertilizer: US growers tend to over-apply nitrogen for a perfect year, and soybeans (a nitrogen-fixing legume) need far less nitrogen, so a squeeze pushes acreage toward soybeans and lighter input crops.
insights.speaker: guest, Bruce Sherrick
insights.so_what: A fertilizer squeeze is a relative bid for soybean over corn acreage — watch the corn/soybean acreage and price ratio as a fertilizer-stress indicator.

themes.theme: Geopolitics, escalation risk, and the food-security red line

insights.claim: Sherrick's biggest red line is not the next crop failing but the conflict escalating beyond a 2-3 party fight: as soon as it spills past US-only headlines into a broader regional war, he becomes 'really really worried' about a prolonged bloody conflict that disrupts energy and food.
insights.speaker: guest, Bruce Sherrick
insights.so_what: The tail risk to monitor is conflict widening (Gulf oil exporters drawn in), not a single bad harvest — escalation headlines are the trigger to re-risk ag/energy/food trades.
insights.quote: as soon as it spills out of the the US being the only headlines that we hear about day to day, then I am really really worried

insights.claim: If the Hormuz closure persists another month or two it would materially impact the SOUTHERN hemisphere's next planting cycle (~6 months out), making that the next real shortage-risk window rather than the current northern-hemisphere harvest.
insights.speaker: guest, Bruce Sherrick
insights.so_what: Sets a concrete monitoring clock: a multi-month Hormuz closure is the catalyst for southern-hemisphere (Brazil/Argentina) input and crop risk in the following cycle.
insights.quote: If it goes on another month or two, then it begins to really really impact southern hemisphere's next round of planting

insights.claim: Real food-shortage pockets are emerging for the first time in a long time in parts of Africa and the western part of Asia that depend on imported food — driven by both physical scarcity and unaffordable prices ('you can starve people with prices just the same way you can starve people with lack of food').
insights.speaker: guest, Bruce Sherrick
insights.so_what: Localized food-insecurity / social-unrest risk (the 'Arab Spring' analog) is concentrated in import-dependent emerging markets, not developed economies — an EM political-risk signal.
insights.quote: you can starve people with prices just the same way you can starve people with lack of food

insights.claim: For roughly a third of the world's population, a marginal dollar of income changes what they eat tomorrow, whereas in the US food is a low enough share of income that food inflation is barely noticed despite being a big component of the recent 3.8% inflation print.
insights.speaker: guest, Bruce Sherrick
insights.so_what: Food inflation's social/political impact is asymmetric and concentrated in poor importing nations — a leading indicator for EM instability, not for US consumer behavior.
insights.quote: For about a third of the world's population, if you gave them each another dollar, they might change what they eat tomorrow

insights.claim: Iran has already demonstrated willingness to destroy energy infrastructure (including natural gas) of neighbors, and because everything in food production depends on energy, attacks that raise energy costs erode the value/affordability of all downstream food inputs.
insights.speaker: guest, Bruce Sherrick
insights.so_what: Energy-infrastructure attacks are the transmission mechanism from geopolitics to food costs (natural gas to ammonia) — a key escalation channel to watch.

insights.claim: Even if the war ended and Hormuz reopened tomorrow, productivity and output would not snap back to the prior path — like a temporary smoker, the system permanently loses some of the gains and resources wasted during the disruption.
insights.speaker: guest, Bruce Sherrick
insights.so_what: Reopening is not a full reversal — there's a permanent output 'gap' left on the table, so a peace-deal relief rally may overstate the recovery.
insights.quote: you don't go all the way back up to the same line

themes.theme: Trade rerouting and depressed US commodity prices

insights.claim: Tariffs and geopolitical conflict have already begun 'unspinning' global ag trade — e.g., China buying soybeans from Argentina/Brazil instead of the US, forcing US exporters to backfill more distant markets at much higher transportation cost borne by the seller.
insights.speaker: guest, Bruce Sherrick
insights.so_what: Structurally bearish for US soybean prices and grower margins; bullish relative positioning for South American exporters — a durable trade-reordering theme.
insights.quote: when China decides to buy from Argentina and Brazil and not from the US and the US has to then go fill in backfill the rest of the world demand for soybeans to places it didn't used to sell

insights.claim: Lower US commodity prices are likely to PERSIST even if China meets soybean-purchase obligations under trade agreements, because the expensive rerouting of global trade is paid for largely by the seller.
insights.speaker: guest, Bruce Sherrick
insights.so_what: Lean bearish on US grain prices/grower income into the trade-reordering regime even on headline 'China is buying' news.
insights.quote: lower prices are likely to persist in the US

insights.claim: Sherrick is as worried about depressed US output prices from trade rerouting as he is about next year's crop physically not being produced, since output prices ultimately drive planting decisions.
insights.speaker: guest, Bruce Sherrick
insights.so_what: Reframes the dominant risk from supply-side famine to demand/price-side margin compression for US producers.
insights.quote: So I'm as worried about that as I am about next year's crop not being able to be produced

insights.claim: Comparative advantage means the US cannot economically replace interrupted fertilizer supply just because it has cheap, sometimes-flared natural gas — artificial trade barriers (tariffs) are 'really sloppy taxing' and natural gas is most valuable in its current uses.
insights.speaker: guest, Bruce Sherrick
insights.so_what: Skeptical of a US fertilizer-onshoring thesis as an economic winner; tariffs raise system-wide costs rather than create durable domestic advantage.
insights.quote: it's just really sloppy taxing

themes.theme: Second-order effects: steel, equipment, processing chain

insights.claim: Steel and aluminum tariffs may matter more to farm economics over time than fertilizer prices because they permanently raise the cost of durable equipment — a combine that cost ~$1 million could cost ~$1.2 million, and that elevated cost lasts the machine's entire useful life.
insights.speaker: guest, Bruce Sherrick
insights.so_what: A long-duration, under-appreciated cost drag on farm capex and margins — bearish for farmer economics and a margin/pricing variable for equipment makers like John Deere.
insights.quote: a combine that used to cost a million might be a million too because of the increased cost of components

insights.claim: Wheat itself is only ~10-15% of the cost of a loaf of bread, with transportation and packaging each a similar share, so the more processed a food and the slower the economy turns, the more all energy-linked input costs compound into the final consumer price.
insights.speaker: guest, Bruce Sherrick
insights.so_what: Food-price inflation is driven more by energy-linked processing/transport/packaging than by raw grain prices — track energy and logistics, not just crop futures, for consumer food CPI.
insights.quote: If you take wheat and say per bushel how much of the you know value of a bread a loaf of bread is represented by the input of wheat it's a really small fraction 10 15% maybe

insights.claim: Plastics and other oil-derived inputs (for wrapping, cooling, freezing) mean the food system's energy exposure runs far beyond diesel — the most-processed foods carry the highest embedded energy cost and erode most when energy prices rise.
insights.speaker: guest, Bruce Sherrick
insights.so_what: Highly-processed/packaged food categories are most exposed to energy-cost inflation — relevant for packaged-food company margins.

insights.claim: A smaller US beef herd will keep beef prices elevated until the herd is rebuilt, a dynamic largely separate from the fertilizer/energy shock but additive to food-price stickiness.
insights.speaker: guest, Bruce Sherrick
insights.so_what: Beef prices stay structurally high on herd-cycle dynamics independent of the Hormuz event — a separate persistent food-inflation component.
insights.quote: we have a smaller beef herd right now. So beef prices will stay up until we have a bigger beef herd

themes.theme: Named companies and episodic fertilizer trade

insights.claim: Nutrien and adjacent fertilizer/ag-input companies should benefit like energy companies from this shock — when competing suppliers are disrupted, the remaining producers gain, delivering at least a couple of strong quarters even if the long-run normalizes.
insights.speaker: guest, Bruce Sherrick
insights.so_what: Direct long idea: fertilizer producers (Nutrien) are positioned for an episodic earnings pop from disrupted competitors — trade the episode, don't marry it.
insights.quote: Will Nutrient go out of business? No. They'll have a great year.

insights.claim: Fertilizer/ag-input names are 'episodic' beneficiaries — they get a pop when competing suppliers suffer, but the durable economic rents from these episodes ultimately accrue back to land (Ricardo), not to the input companies.
insights.speaker: guest, Bruce Sherrick
insights.so_what: Frames fertilizer equities as tactical/episodic trades versus farmland as the structural rent-capturing asset — different holding periods and theses.
insights.quote: these are um what I would call episodic

insights.claim: David Hay (frequent Thoughtful Money guest) flagged Nutrien to his followers as a fertilizer/food play, and the host independently surfaces it as a name to watch into this disruption.
insights.speaker: host, Adam Taggart
insights.so_what: Names a specific ticker (Nutrien) being circulated as the consensus fertilizer-shock beneficiary among the channel's analysts.

insights.claim: John Deere will not go out of business and won't be permanently impaired by this — at worst a rough year here and there — implying farm-equipment makers are durable holds through the cycle despite the steel/aluminum cost headwind.
insights.speaker: guest, Bruce Sherrick
insights.so_what: Equipment makers are framed as cyclical-but-durable; weakness is buyable rather than terminal.
insights.quote: will John Deere go out of business? No. Will they have a rough year? Maybe now and then

themes.theme: Farmland as the structural inflation-hedge asset

insights.claim: Farmland has beaten the S&P 500 over every 10-year period since 1970 with lower drawdown, while being positively correlated with inflation and negatively correlated with equities — the structural reason it 'wins' once episodic events wash out is that land is the most fixed factor of production (Ricardo).
insights.speaker: guest, Bruce Sherrick
insights.so_what: Core thesis: farmland as a low-drawdown, inflation-hedging, equity-diversifying core holding that historically outperforms equities over rolling decades.
insights.quote: lower draw down than the S&P any 10-year period since 1970 you'll beat the S&P with farmland investments

insights.claim: The $126 trillion boomer 'great wealth transfer' (closer to 2050 than 2000) is driving an exploding wave of less-informed but highly-motivated demand for farmland as a tax-efficient, inflation-protected, long-term-capital-gains wealth-preservation asset across institutional, SMA, and co-mingled vehicles.
insights.speaker: guest, Bruce Sherrick
insights.so_what: A multi-decade structural demand tailwind ('a dam breaking') for farmland prices and farmland-investment vehicles — early in the cycle per Sherrick.
insights.quote: we're going to transfer $126 trillion dollars to the next generation. My number one inbound inquiry right now is a less informed but more motivated uh person interested in in farmland investments

insights.claim: US farmland is a ~$4 trillion sector with only ~13% debt (sector-wide leverage), and the trend toward ever-larger farms requires bigger capital bases — pointing to growing institutional financialization of land ownership.
insights.speaker: guest, Bruce Sherrick
insights.so_what: Low-leverage, large-and-growing addressable market with a financialization runway — structural setup for institutional capital inflows into farmland funds.
insights.quote: the underleveraged in farmland by owners is only 13% debt in the sector it's only a $4 trillion ion dollar sector too

insights.claim: Farmland prices don't fall even when farmers lose money in a given year because the asset is priced against the expectation of future income, a relationship Sherrick says 'hasn't been broken yet' and won't be in another hundred years.
insights.speaker: guest, Bruce Sherrick
insights.so_what: Farmland's price floor is set by capitalized future income, decoupling it from single-year farm P&L — the resilience case for the asset.
insights.quote: farmland doesn't go down in value even when you lose money as a farmer because the expectation of the future income is what you're pricing it against

insights.claim: Higher conventional fertilizer prices create a relative cost advantage for organic/sustainable farmland, because organic nitrogen sources (e.g., chicken litter, composting) don't reprice with ammonia — strengthening the case for sustainable farmland funds.
insights.speaker: guest, Bruce Sherrick
insights.so_what: Fertilizer-price spikes tilt relative economics toward organic/sustainable operations — a tailwind for sustainably-managed farmland strategies.
insights.quote: You've created a relative advantage to the other production system

insights.claim: Sherrick will stake his reputation that farmland is worth more in 10 years, but explicitly refuses any short-term price call ('Do I think it will be tomorrow? No') given the high economic uncertainty.
insights.speaker: guest, Bruce Sherrick
insights.so_what: High conviction on the decade-out direction, none on near-term timing — farmland is positioned as a patient, long-horizon allocation.
insights.quote: Do I think farmland will be worth more in 10 years? Absolutely... Do I think it will be tomorrow? No

insights.claim: Sherrick believes events like this and COVID are forcing a structural reappraisal of resilience over pure efficiency, meaning the world may need 'more reserves of everything everywhere' — a permanent drag on growth but a durable bid for resilient real assets like farmland.
insights.speaker: guest, Bruce Sherrick
insights.so_what: A regime shift from efficiency to resilience favors holding excess inventory and real assets — supportive for commodities, reserves, and farmland over the long run.
insights.quote: maybe that means we need more reserves of everything everywhere

insights.claim: Genetic improvement is the single biggest driver of agricultural productivity — US corn now yields ~250 bushels/acre where it once yielded 100, and (per former Secretary Vilsack) the US grows three times as much food as ~100 years ago with only half the inputs.
insights.speaker: guest, Bruce Sherrick
insights.so_what: Relentless yield/genetics gains are the structural reason Sherrick says Malthusian famine calls are always wrong — bullish long-run for food supply and ag-tech/seed innovators.
insights.quote: that's why you grow 250 bush of corn where you used to only grow 100

insights.claim: Autonomous and 'see-and-spray' technology is rapidly displacing farm labor and chemical use — nearly all tractors are now self-driving, large new sprayers selectively spray only detected weeds, and laser/robotic weeders (Blue River, others) reduce herbicide/pesticide needs, especially in organic systems.
insights.speaker: guest, Bruce Sherrick
insights.so_what: Structural ag-tech adoption lowers labor and chemical intensity — relevant for precision-ag and autonomy plays and a secular cost-down for growers.
insights.quote: All all tractors are essentially self-driving now

insights.claim: Immigration/labor constraints have 'poured gasoline on the fire' of agricultural automation, accelerating robotic harvesting first in high-value, labor-intensive crops like apples and tree nuts.
insights.speaker: guest, Bruce Sherrick
insights.so_what: Labor scarcity is a catalyst accelerating ag-automation capex — a demand driver for ag-robotics and harvesting-tech firms.
insights.quote: gasoline poured on the fire for that development as well
